title: Economic Bulletin Issue 7, 2025
summary: At its 30 October 2025 meeting, the ECB Governing Council kept its three key interest rates unchanged, citing inflation close to the 2% target and a resilient but uncertain economy. The euro area grew 0.2% in Q3 2025, with services boosted by AI-related IT modernization while manufacturing faced headwinds from tariffs and trade uncertainty. Annual inflation rose to 2.2% in September from 2.0% in August, driven by energy prices.
key_points: ECB Governing Council kept three key interest rates unchanged at 30 October 2025 meeting
Euro area GDP grew 0.2% in Q3 2025 per Eurostat flash estimate; services sector expanded on AI/digital modernization, manufacturing weighed down by tariffs
Annual inflation increased to 2.2% in September 2025 from 2.0% in August; energy price inflation was -0.4% (up from -2.0%)
Unemployment remained at 6.3% in September 2025, near historical lows
Governing Council will follow data-dependent, meeting-by-meeting approach with no pre-commitment to rate path
ECB bank lending rates for firms averaged 3.5% in August 2025; annual growth rate of bank lending to firms edged down to 2.9% in September
ECB decided to move to next stage of digital euro project at 30 October 2025 meeting to ensure technical readiness
Forward-looking wage indicators point to slower wage growth over remainder of 2025 and first half of 2026
why_it_matters: This Economic Bulletin provides the ECB's comprehensive assessment of euro area economic conditions and monetary policy rationale. Market participants monitoring ECB policy direction will note the unchanged rates and data-dependent stance, with the Governing Council emphasizing resilience from past rate cuts and labor market strength while flagging trade and geopolitical uncertainty as key risks. The bulletin signals the ECB is in a watchful mode, neither signaling imminent cuts nor hikes.
macro_context: The euro area shows modest growth (0.2% Q3) with divergent sectoral performance—services thriving on AI-driven IT investment while manufacturing contracts on tariff impacts. Labor market remains tight at 6.3% unemployment. Inflation ticked up to 2.2% but underlying measures remain consistent with the 2% target. Key risks include global trade disputes, geopolitical tensions (particularly Russia-Ukraine war), and potential supply chain disruptions. EU-US trade deal and Middle East ceasefire have partially mitigated downside risks. Forward-looking wage growth expected to moderate through H1 2026.
market_structure_context: The bulletin mentions the ECB's decision to advance the digital euro project to the next stage at the 30 October meeting, supporting Europe's digital sovereignty once legislation is adopted. The Governing Council notes commitment to making retail and wholesale central bank money fit for the digital age.
notes: Source is an official ECB Economic Bulletin, a high-quality institutional publication. Published 5 months before TODAY, within the 90-day window. The document was truncated at end but contains sufficient content for comprehensive analysis. The source clearly falls within macro catalysts topic as it discusses ECB policy, inflation dynamics, growth outlook, and risk assessment.
